sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 96, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 95 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 3.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After pressing RIGHT:
- Left feet: moved from cols 9-13 to cols 14-18 (right 5 cols)
- Right feet: moved from cols 49-53 to cols 44-48 (left 5 cols)
So both feet moved. The right feet were NOT blocked from moving left when at cols 49-53. Earlier, the right feet were blocked from moving left when at cols 44-48. This suggests that the right feet can move between cols 44-48 and cols 49-53, but not beyond cols 44-48 to the left.
So the horizontal range for the feet is:
- Left feet: cols 9-13, 14-18, 19-23 (3 positions)
- Right feet: cols 39-43, 44-48, 49-53 (3 positions)
Wait, but I need to verify this. Let me check if the left feet can move to cols 19-23 by pressing RIGHT again.
Current feet: rows 39-43, cols 14-18 (left) and rows 39-43, cols 44-48 (right).
If I press RIGHT:
- Left feet would move right to cols 19-23
- Right feet would try to move left to cols 39-43, but might be blocked at cols 44-48
Actually, earlier when the right feet were at cols 44-48 and I pressed RIGHT, they stayed at cols 44-48 (blocked). So pressing RIGHT now would move the left feet to cols 19-23, and the right feet would stay at cols 44-48.
This is getting complicated. Let me think about what the goal might be.
Looking at the initial state:
- Left feet: rows 19-23, cols 19-23
- Right feet: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
The left feet started at cols 19-23 (center), and the right feet started at cols 44-48 (center-left of right body).
Maybe the goal is to have the feet at the "hands" at rows 29-38, cols 14-18 (left) and cols 49-53 (right). This would be a symmetric position at the ends of the arms.
Current feet: rows 39-43, cols 14-18 (left) and rows 39-43, cols 44-48 (right).
To get to the goal:
- Move both feet up from rows 39-43 to rows 29-38: 4-14 rows up
- Left feet are already at cols 14-18 (correct)
- Right feet need to move from cols 44-48 to cols 49-53: 5 cols right
To move the right feet right, I press LEFT. But pressing LEFT would also move the left feet left (from 14-18 to 9-13). But the left feet are already at the correct cols 14-18, so I don't want to move them left.
Wait, but earlier when the left feet were at cols 14-18 and I pressed LEFT, they moved to cols 9-13. So the left feet are not blocked from moving left at cols 14-18.
Hmm, this is tricky. I need to move the right feet right (press LEFT) without moving the left feet left. But the actions affect both feet simultaneously.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the earlier behavior:
- When left feet at cols 9-13 and I pressed RIGHT, left feet moved to 14-18, right feet stayed at 44-48 (blocked).
- When left feet at cols 14-18 and I pressed LEFT, left feet moved to 9-13, right feet moved to 49-53.
- When right feet at cols 49-53 and I pressed RIGHT, left feet moved to 14-18, right feet moved to 44-48.
So the blocking behavior is:
- Right feet at cols 44-48 are blocked from moving left (pressing RIGHT doesn't move them).
- Right feet at cols 49-53 can move left (pressing RIGHT moves them to 44-48).
- Left feet at cols 14-18 can move left (pressing LEFT moves them to 9-13).
- Left feet at cols 9-13 are blocked from moving left (pressing LEFT doesn't move them).
So the horizontal range is:
- Left feet: cols 9-13, 14-18, 19-23 (3 positions)
- Right feet: cols 39-43, 44-48, 49-53 (3 positions)
And the blocking is at the edges:
- Left feet at cols 9-13 are blocked from moving left.
- Right feet at cols 44-48 are blocked from moving left.
Wait, but the right feet at cols 44-48 are blocked from moving left, not from moving right. Let me re-examine.
When right feet at cols 44-48 and I pressed RIGHT, they stayed at cols 44-48. RIGHT moves right feet left, so they were blocked from moving left. This means the right feet cannot move to cols 39-43.
So the horizontal range for the right feet is cols 44-48 and cols 49-53 (2 positions), not 3 positions.
And for the left feet:
- When left feet at cols 9-13 and I pressed LEFT, they stayed at cols 9-13. LEFT moves left feet left, so they were blocked from moving left. This means the left feet cannot move to cols 4-8.
- When left feet at cols 14-18 and I pressed RIGHT, they moved to cols 19-23. So the left feet can move to cols 19-23.
So the horizontal range for the left feet is cols 9-13, 14-18, 19-23 (3 positions).
Now, the goal might be to have the feet at cols 14-18 (left) and cols 49-53 (right), which are the "hand" positions. But I can't move the right feet to cols 49-53 without moving the left feet from cols 14-18 to cols 9-13.
Wait, but maybe I can use the blocking behavior. If the left feet are at cols 9-13 (blocked from moving left), and I press LEFT, the left feet stay at cols 9-13, and the right feet move right. Let me test this.
Current feet: rows 39-43, cols 14-18 (left) and rows 39-43, cols 44-48 (right).
1. Press LEFT: left feet move to 9-13, right feet move to 49-53.
2. Press LEFT: left feet stay at 9-13 (blocked), right feet stay at 49-53 (blocked from moving right? or can they move further right?).
Actually, I'm not sure if the right feet are blocked from moving right at cols 49-53. Let me test this.
But wait, I think I'm overcomplicating this. Let me just try to get the feet to the "hand" positions at rows 29-38, cols 14-18 (left) and cols 49-53 (right), and then press SPACE to see if it completes the level.
Current feet: rows 39-43, cols 14-18 (left) and rows 39-43, cols 44-48 (right).
1. Press LEFT: left feet move to 9-13, right feet move to 49-53.
2. Press RIGHT: left feet move to 14-18, right feet stay at 49-53 (blocked from moving left).
3. Press UP 10 times: move feet to rows 29-33.
4. Press SPACE: see if it completes the level.
Let me try this sequence.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current feet: rows 39-43, cols 14-18 (left) and rows 39-43, cols 44-48 (right)
# Goal: get feet to rows 29-38, cols 14-18 (left) and cols 49-53 (right)
# Strategy:
# 1. LEFT: left feet 14-18 -> 9-13, right feet 44-48 -> 49-53
# 2. RIGHT: left feet 9-13 -> 14-18, right feet 49-53 -> blocked (stay at 49-53)
# 3. UP x 10: move feet to rows 29-33
# 4. SPACE: test if level completes

action(['LEFT', 'RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in node['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in node['boundary']]
        print(f"Feet: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Feet: rows 39-43, cols 14-18
Feet: rows 39-43, cols 44-48
